summary: unsupported_context_too_large: context (7460494 chars) exceeds shim cap (480000)

test_tcl failed with 2 test failures: test_eval_surrogates_in_result and test_evalfile_surrogates_in_result. Both tests expect emoji characters from TCL eval/evalfile operations to be properly decoded as Unicode characters, but instead receive UTF-16 surrogate pairs.
root_cause: TCL surrogate pair decoding failure: When TCL eval() and evalfile() operations return strings containing non-BMP Unicode characters (like U+1F4BB, the laptop emoji), the Python TCL bindings are returning UTF-16 surrogate pairs (\ud83d\udcbb) instead of properly decoding them to the corresponding Unicode character. The test expects '<\U0001f4bb>' but receives '<\ud83d\udcbb>'.

evidence.reason: Direct assertion failure showing surrogate pairs returned instead of properly decoded Unicode character

evidence.quote: File "D:\a\cpython\cpython\Lib\test\test_tcl.py", line 57, in test_eval_surrogates_in_result: self.assertEqual(tcl.eval(r'set a "<\ud83d\udcbb>"'), '<\U0001f4bb>')
evidence.reason: Test explicitly expects TCL eval to return U+1F4BB character properly decoded

evidence.quote: File "D:\a\cpython\cpython\Lib\test\test_tcl.py", line 292, in test_evalfile_surrogates_in_result: self.assertEqual(tcl.eval('set b'), '<\U0001f4bb>')
evidence.reason: Identical issue in evalfile_surrogates test at line 292
Failure is isolated to TCL module; other 455 tests passed successfully

suggested_fix: Investigate the TCL-Python character encoding bridge in Modules/_tkinter.c to ensure surrogate pairs returned by TCL operations are properly decoded to their corresponding Unicode characters before being returned to Python, particularly for characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane.

Segmentation fault in numpy's argsort function while processing datetime NaT values during test_datetime_nat_argsort_stability test execution.
root_cause: Runtime segmentation fault in numpy argsort C implementation when handling datetime NaT (Not-a-Time) values, causing a fatal Python error and process exit with code 245.

evidence.reason: Explicit fatal segmentation fault reported by Python runtime
Stack trace shows the failure originates in the argsort function
Test case name explicitly indicates this is an argsort stability test with datetime NaT values
Exit code 245 typically indicates a signal-based crash (segmentation fault)

suggested_fix: Investigate and fix the segmentation fault in numpy's argsort implementation when handling datetime NaT values. The crash occurs at the C code level during the sorting operation on datetime arrays containing NaT (Not-a-Time) elements. Review recent changes to argsort, sorting algorithms, or datetime handling in the core numpy code.
